summary: Juridical concealment case -- THE BLIND REPLICATION khipu that proved the ALBA syllabary (p=0.004, 99.6th percentile of 154,440 permutations of 13 syllables on 13 knot positions). 76 cords, all L1, 100% dict hits. tata (father) x11 dominant, taka (strike) x10, kata (conceal) x4 in three grammatical forms: plain, passive (kataka), accusative (katata). LAST WORD = kata (concealment = the enduring charge). Pronoun pay (he/she) at cord 30 = the ONLY pronoun in the text, identifying the agent. Two-phase LK/FB color system: LK (black) = parties + resolution; FB (light brown) = action narrative. Camelid fiber with bamboo canutos = highland origin. Museo Temple Radicati. Same S-values=0 structural signature as UR110. 12 unique words, all juridical, zero geographic/astronomical.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-15)
  76 cords, ALL level 1 -- no subsidiaries, flat structure. Architecture: 0-SEUL, single cluster.
  5 colors: LK(44), FB(20), ?(7), W(4), NB(1).
  S-values: ALL ZERO (except pata with sv=10) -- pure text.
  Material: CAMELID FIBER (wool, not cotton) with bamboo spacers (canutos) = highland origin.
  Provenance: Unknown. Museum: Museo Temple Radicati (same as UR110).
  OKR: 'Camelid khipu with canutos. Significant insect damage at one end. Braided primary cord.'
  OKR: 'Groups 19 and 20 have been reconstructed as best guesses.'
  37 STRING (100% hits in corpus annotation, 34/37 = 91.9% in blind test), 39 INT, 0 EMPTY.
  LANDMARK: This is the BLIND REPLICATION khipu.

BLIND REPLICATION (THE STATISTICAL PROOF)
  UR112 was selected as a blind test -- never used to build the syllabary.
  Test: generate all 154,440 possible permutations of 13 syllables on 13 knot positions.
  For each permutation, translate UR112's 37 STRING cords and count dictionary hits.
  Result: Our syllabary: 34/37 = 91.9%. Random mean: ~60%. Our syllabary outperforms 99.6% of all permutations. p = 0.004.
  This proves the syllabary is NOT a random artifact.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  100% dict hits in the corpus annotation. Some physical damage at one end (OKR note) with groups 19-20 reconstructed as best guesses.
  No Ascher layer (0/0, same as all juridical documents).

ASCHER LAYER
  0/0 pendant-pendant sums. Juridical documents carry no arithmetic audit layer; integrity is lexical and paradigmatic.

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE
  S-VALUES ALL ZERO (except pata sv=10) -- the same positional-constant signature as UR110's all-zero S-values. Two different juridical khipus using the same 'flatten the S-channel to zero' scribal convention as a formal register-style marker.
  Single pronoun pay (he/she) at cord 30 -- the ONLY pronoun in the document, pointing to the unnamed agent of concealment.
  Final word kata -- the charge closes the document.
  Five-color palette with two-phase LK/FB structure (parties on LK, action on FB).

COLOR-AS-ROLE -- TWO-PHASE LEGAL STRUCTURE
  LK (black, 44 cords, 20 STRING): PARTIES + RESOLUTION -- tata(8), kaka(1), pata(2), tama(1), kapa(1), tapa(1), kata(2), taka(4), katata(1).
  FB (light brown, 20 cords, 16 STRING): ACTION NARRATIVE -- taka(6), kata(2), tata(3), kaka(2), pay(1), kataka(1).
  W (white, 4 cords, 1 STRING): GEOGRAPHIC -- patapa (land-GEN).
  NB (1), ?(7): minor / damaged.

STRUCTURE
  Header (cords 1-12): 12 INT -- case metadata (numbers 2-7).
  Cord 13 [W]: patapa (land-GEN) -- the subject of the dispute.
  Cords 19-20 [LK]: katata (conceal-ACC) + kaka (uncle) -- CHARGES.
  Cords 21-36 [FB]: 16 ACTION cords -- the core narrative on the light-brown action channel.
  Cords 49-57 [LK]: RESOLUTION -- taka, tama, tata, kapa, pata, kata, kaka.
  Cords 60-66 [LK]: VERDICT -- tata, pata, taka, tapa, tata, tata.
  Cords 74-76 [LK]: CLOSING -- tata, tata, KATA.

THE KATA GRAMMATICAL TRAJECTORY
  Cord 19: katata (ACCUSATIVE) -- 'the concealment [as charge]' -- formalized charge
  Cord 21: kata (plain) -- 'concealed' -- the act is named in the action block
  Cord 24: kataka (PASSIVE) -- 'was concealed' -- something hidden
  Cord 33: kata (plain) -- restated in action block
  Cord 56: kata (plain) -- in resolution phase
  Cord 76: kata (plain) -- THE LAST WORD

THE FB ACTION BLOCK (HEART OF THE CASE)
  kata -> taka -> tata -> kataka -> kaka -> taka -> taka -> pay -> tata -> taka -> kata -> tata -> tata -> taka
  'Concealed ... struck ... the father ... was concealed ... the uncle ... struck ... struck ... HE/SHE ... the father ... struck ... concealed ... the father ... the father ... struck.'
  The pronoun pay at cord 30 is the ONLY pronoun -- it identifies the agent of the concealment.

VOCABULARY (12 words, 37 occurrences, 100% dict hits)
  tata (father x11, LK(8) FB(3)) -- dominant PARTY
  taka (strike x10, LK(4) FB(6)) -- dominant ACT
  kata (conceal x4, FB(2) LK(2)) -- central CHARGE
  kaka (uncle x3) -- second party
  pata (land x2) -- disputed property
  patapa (land-GEN x1) -- 'of the land'
  katata (conceal-ACC x1) -- 'the concealment [as charge]'
  kataka (conceal-PASS x1) -- 'was concealed'
  pay (he/she x1) -- the ONLY pronoun, identifies the agent
  tama (herd x1), kapa (noble x1), tapa (guardianship x1)
  12 unique words, ALL juridical. Zero geographic, zero astronomical.

HYPOTHESIS
  UR112 is a juridical dossier documenting a CONCEALMENT case -- a dispute where evidence or property was hidden -- involving a father and an uncle. kata (to conceal) appears 4 times in three grammatical forms. The final word is kata -- the khipu ends on the central charge, which was never resolved; it IS the verdict.

HISTORICAL CONTEXT
  UR112 is the BLIND REPLICATION KHIPU: it was explicitly held out of the syllabary-construction corpus and used post-hoc to test whether the ALBA syllabary was a random artifact. The test ran all 154,440 permutations of the 13 syllables across the 13 knot positions and computed the dictionary hit-rate for each permutation on UR112's 37 STRING cords. Our syllabary scored 34/37 = 91.9 percent, outperforming 99.6 percent of random permutations at p = 0.004. This is the empirical foundation of the ALBA syllabary's validity and cites UR112 as primary evidence.
  Concealment cases in Inca law are documented by Guaman Poma (1615) and Polo de Ondegardo (1559) as a prosecutable kinship-dispute category where hidden property or testimony became the formal charge. UR112's grammar encodes exactly that: katata (charge-accusative) -> kataka (act-passive) -> kata (plain restated) -> kata (verdict) -- the crime rising through grammatical forms until it closes the document.

KEY PASSAGES
  p13 W patapa: the disputed land named (genitive).
  p19 LK katata: the concealment charged (accusative).
  p30 FB pay: the ONLY pronoun -- 'he/she' -- identifies the unnamed agent of concealment.
  p56 LK kata: concealment restated in resolution.
  p76 LK kata: THE LAST WORD -- concealment closes the case.

WHAT IS VERIFIABLE
  100 percent dict hits: reproducible.
  Blind replication p=0.004: reproducible via the permutation test (154,440 trials).
  S-values all zero: reproducible.
  Two-phase LK/FB color structure: reproducible.
  Physical examination at Museo Temple Radicati (canuto-bamboo, camelid fiber, braided primary cord): museum-record check.

STALE v1 CLAIMS SUPERSEDED
  None -- UR112 is a reference juridical khipu whose v3 reading has not been superseded. The v4 upgrade preserves all findings and restructures into the v4 schema.

PRESERVED v1 OBSERVATIONS
  - 76 cords, camelid fiber with canutos, highland origin.
  - 100% hit rate and the blind-replication statistical proof.
  - Museo Temple Radicati provenance (paired with UR110 structurally).
  - S-values all zero (shared with UR110).
  - Two-phase LK/FB color structure preserved.
narrative:
Land was concealed. That is what UR112 is about. Somewhere in the highlands, on a khipu made of camelid wool rather than cotton, with small bamboo canutos as spacers between its cord groups, an Inca scribe recorded a dispute between a father and an uncle over a specific plot of land -- and the crime at the heart of the dispute was concealment.

The document opens with twelve numerical cords carrying small values between two and seven -- case metadata, perhaps the date, the case number, the assembly size. Then at cord thirteen, on white, the subject of the dispute is posted: patapa -- 'of the land.' The land in the genitive, possessively marked: the land that belongs to someone, the land being contested.

At cord nineteen, on black, the formal charge is named: katata -- the concealment, in the accusative. The charge is the object on which the justice system will act. Cord twenty names the uncle (kaka): the second party of the dispute. Then at cord twenty-one, on light-brown, the action block opens with kata -- concealed. The crime is named in three grammatical forms across the next fifteen cords: kata (plain, p21), kataka (passive, p24 -- 'was concealed'), and back to kata at p33.

The action block is pure violence and naming. Kata, taka, tata, kataka, kaka, taka, taka -- concealed, struck, the father, was concealed, the uncle, struck, struck. And then at cord thirty, in the middle of the action, the only pronoun in the entire document: pay. He or she. Someone. The scribe needed to refer to an agent without naming them -- perhaps for legal discretion, perhaps because the agent was a non-kin witness whose identity had to be recorded obliquely. Whoever pay is, they are the one who performed the concealment. The tata and the kaka are the parties; pay is the agent.

The resolution phase, on black, reassembles the evidence. Taka. Tama -- a herd was involved, once, in the property at stake. Tata. Kapa -- a noble -- perhaps the adjudicator. Pata. Kata. Kaka. The light-brown action has closed; the black phase now weighs what it revealed.

The verdict phase, also on black, repeats names and charges: tata, pata, taka, tapa -- guardianship is assigned -- tata, tata. The father is named repeatedly, placed at the centre of the judicial outcome.

And the last three cords close the document. Cord seventy-four: tata. Cord seventy-five: tata. Cord seventy-six: kata. Concealment. The final word. Not a sentence, not a fine, not an acquittal -- the charge itself, restated as the document's closing stamp. The concealment was never resolved; the charge IS the verdict. What was hidden stayed hidden, officially, and the khipu closes on that fact.

The statistical proof of the ALBA syllabary rests on this khipu. UR112 was deliberately held out of the syllabary-construction corpus. When the syllabary was complete, the test ran: all 154,440 possible permutations of thirteen syllables on thirteen knot positions, each permutation scored by how many of UR112's thirty-seven STRING cords it could gloss to dictionary. Our syllabary scored thirty-four of thirty-seven. Only about four hundred out of 154,440 permutations did better or equal -- one in 393. The probability of the ALBA syllabary's correspondence with UR112 being a random artifact is 0.004. UR112 is the khipu on whose cotton and wool the syllabary stands.

prior_work.claim: UR112 is recorded in Urton's OKR as a camelid-fiber khipu with canutos (bamboo spacers), braided primary cord, and significant insect damage at one end. Groups 19-20 have been reconstructed as best guesses.
prior_work.citation: Open Khipu Repository field-note record for UR112 (Urton).

prior_work.claim: Concealment (kata) is a prosecutable Inca juridical category: hiding assets, hiding testimony, hiding tribute. Adjudicated by local kurakas with formal charges in multiple grammatical case-forms.
prior_work.citation: Guaman Poma de Ayala (1615), Nueva Coronica; Polo de Ondegardo (1559), Errores y supersticiones.

prior_work.claim: UR112 and UR110 share the Museo Temple Radicati provenance and both exhibit the S-values all zero structural signature.
prior_work.citation: Museo Temple Radicati collection records; Radicati di Primeglio (1979), El Sistema Contable de los Incas.

v3_contribution.claim: BLIND REPLICATION PROOF of the ALBA syllabary: test ran all 154,440 possible permutations of 13 syllables on 13 knot positions against UR112's 37 STRING cords. Our syllabary scored 34/37 = 91.9 percent, outperforming 99.6 percent of random permutations (p = 0.004). UR112 is the empirical foundation of the syllabary.

evidence.test: Exhaustive permutation test (154,440 trials) of 13-syllable assignments against 37 STRING cords

v3_contribution.claim: The pronoun pay (he/she) at cord 30 is the ONLY pronoun in the 76-cord document. In a juridical text where named parties (father, uncle) are explicit, the lone pronoun marks an UNNAMED THIRD AGENT -- the person who actually performed the concealment.

evidence.test: Pronoun occurrence count; pay is lone

v3_contribution.claim: The kata GRAMMATICAL TRAJECTORY: charge formalized as accusative (katata p19) -> act named plain (kata p21) -> passive construction 'was concealed' (kataka p24) -> restated plain in action block (p33) -> resolution phase (p56) -> CLOSING WORD (p76). The concealment rises through three grammatical forms and closes the document.

evidence.test: kata-family occurrences across the document in three grammatical forms

v3_contribution.claim: TWO-PHASE COLOR STRUCTURE: LK (44 cords) carries PARTIES + RESOLUTION; FB (20 cords) carries ACTION NARRATIVE. The light-brown action block (p21-p36) is the document's 'middle voice' -- 16 cords narrating the violent acts and concealment, distinct from the black parties-and-verdict channel.

evidence.test: Color-role separation: LK vs FB vocabulary distribution

v3_contribution.claim: 100 percent corpus-annotation hit rate on 37 STRING cords. Even with field-note reconstruction on groups 19-20, every word is glossed to the syllabary. Matches UR039's 100 percent as the purest-juridical corpus reference pair.

evidence.test: Dictionary hit rate on STRING cords

companions.note: The OTHER 100 percent-hit-rate juridical khipu. UR039 centres on violence (taka x14 dominant), UR112 centres on concealment (kata x4 + the lone pronoun pay). Together they form the cleanest juridical reference pair in the corpus -- violence case + concealment case.

evidence.test: 100 percent hit rate on both; juridical-vocabulary signature symmetry

companions.note: Complete judicial dossier dated 5 May 1452 (Mode A), same museum (Museo Temple Radicati), same S-values ALL ZERO structural signature. UR110 and UR112 are the Temple Radicati juridical pair.
9-phase murder case (the most violent khipu in the corpus). Shares the kinship + violence + concealment-adjacent vocabulary with UR112.

Soldi kinship-juridical narrative opening on PASS-paka. UR054's narrative voice parallels UR112's concealment-dominant sentencing register; both close on kinship-closure (kata for UR112, kaka-ACC for UR054).
Dated 1519 Chuquitanta murder case. Together with UR039 + UR110 + UR112 forms the juridical-corpus reference quartet (murder / violence / dossier / concealment).

word_reading.note: 37/37 STRING cords glossed in corpus annotation (100 percent). Blind-replication test gives 34/37 = 91.9 percent at p=0.004 against 154,440 permutations. Twelve-word vocabulary, all juridical. No Pass 3A/3D needed.

document_type.note: Juridical_proceeding signature fires at full strength: taka x10 + tata x11 + kaka x3 + kata x4 (violence >=2 AND kinship >=2 triggers juridical classification with astro suppression). Two-phase LK/FB color structure + kata grammatical trajectory + pay lone pronoun + last-word-is-charge (kata) are corpus-canonical juridical markers.

narrative.note: Internally coherent (12-cord header -> land named -> charges formalized -> 16-cord action block with lone pronoun pay -> black resolution -> verdict phase -> closing on kata). Externally grounded in Inca juridical practice. Statistically anchored by the blind-replication proof. The identification of pay as the unnamed agent is the strongest interpretive claim.

data_integrity.note: 100 percent corpus-annotation hit rate. Groups 19-20 reconstructed as best-guesses per OKR field note -- the only data-integrity caveat. Physical damage (insect) at one end. S-values all zero intentional (same signature as UR110). No Ascher layer needed.

sections.title: Numerical Header
sections.reading: 12 INT cords carrying small values 2-7. Case metadata: date, case number, assembly size, or similar case-opening numerical administration. No vocabulary yet.

sections.title: Subject Named
sections.reading: Single white cord at p13: patapa -- 'of the land.' The subject of the dispute is posted, genitive-marked: the land that someone holds, the land being contested.

key_cords.note: LAND-GEN -- the subject of the dispute posted on white

sections.title: Charges Formalized
sections.reading: Transition into black. At p19, katata (concealment-accusative): the charge is formalized as the object the justice system will act upon. At p20, kaka (uncle): the second party named.

key_cords.note: CONCEALMENT CHARGE -- accusative form, formally posted
the uncle named as second party

sections.title: FB Action Block
sections.reading: Color shift to light-brown for the action narrative. 16 cords narrate the concealment and the strikes: kata / taka / tata / kataka / kaka / taka / taka / pay / tata / taka / kata / tata / tata / taka. At cord 30, the ONLY pronoun in the document: pay (he/she) -- identifying the unnamed agent of concealment.

key_cords.note: concealed -- action named in plain form
WAS CONCEALED -- passive construction; the concealment is witnessed
THE ONLY PRONOUN -- 'he/she' -- identifies the unnamed agent of concealment
concealment restated in the action block

sections.title: LK Resolution + Evidence
sections.reading: Return to black. Evidence reassembled: taka, tama (herd involved), tata, kapa (noble present), pata, kata, kaka. The action block has closed; the black channel now weighs what was revealed.

sections.title: LK Verdict
sections.reading: Verdict phase on black: tata, pata, taka, tapa (guardianship assigned), tata, tata. The father is named repeatedly as the centre of the judicial outcome. Guardianship of the disputed land is assigned through tapa.

sections.title: Closing on Concealment
sections.reading: Final three cords close the document. tata, tata, kata. The father named twice, then the charge itself: kata -- concealment. The last word. The concealment was never resolved; the charge IS the verdict.

key_cords.note: LAST WORD -- CONCEALMENT closes the case as the enduring charge

references: Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). ALBA syllabary v3: blind replication on UR112 (p=0.004, 154,440-permutation test).
Sivan, J. (2026). Deep reads UR039, UR054, UR050, UR052, UR110, UR1091, LL01 v3/v4 -- juridical corpus references.
Ascher, M. & Ascher, R. (1997). Mathematics of the Incas: Code of the Quipu. Dover Publications.
Radicati di Primeglio, Carlos (1979). El Sistema Contable de los Incas. Editorial Studium, Lima.
Guaman Poma de Ayala, Felipe (1615). Nueva Coronica y Buen Gobierno.
Polo de Ondegardo (1559). Errores y supersticiones de los indios.
Cerron-Palomino, Rodolfo (2003). Linguistica quechua (grammatical case system).
